text:
CT SCAN OF THE CHEST, ABDOMEN, AND PELVIS WITH INTRAVENOUS CONTRAST
SURVEILLANCE IMAGING

PATIENT: Margaret Osei-Bonsu
INDICATION: Surveillance imaging for high-grade serous ovarian carcinoma, FIGO stage IIIC, germline BRCA1 pathogenic variant. Status post primary debulking surgery with R0 resection, followed by six cycles of carboplatin/paclitaxel chemotherapy with complete biochemical and radiographic response. Currently on niraparib (Zejula) maintenance therapy, dose-reduced to 100 mg daily. This examination represents the 24-month post-chemotherapy completion surveillance CT, approximately 28 months from initial diagnosis. Prior CA-125 values have remained within the normal range. Comparison made to prior surveillance CT examination performed approximately 12 months ago (at 12 months post-chemotherapy completion) and to the immediate post-treatment restaging scan.

TECHNIQUE: Multidetector CT of the chest, abdomen, and pelvis was performed following the intravenous administration of 100 mL of iohexol nonionic contrast material, injected at a rate of 2.5 mL/sec via right antecubital access. Images were acquired in the portal venous phase through the abdomen and pelvis, and in the venous phase through the chest. Axial reconstructions at 3 mm slice thickness were supplemented by coronal and sagittal reformats at 3 mm. Oral contrast was not administered per departmental protocol for oncologic surveillance. Dose modulation employed. No adverse reaction to contrast material.

---

FINDINGS:

CHEST:

Lungs and airways: The lungs are clear bilaterally. No pulmonary nodules, masses, or consolidations are identified. The airways are patent to the subsegmental level bilaterally. No evidence of lymphangitic carcinomatosis. No ground-glass opacities or interstitial abnormality.

Pleura: No pleural effusion, pleural thickening, or pleural nodularity is identified bilaterally. The costophrenic angles are sharp and well-defined.

Mediastinum and hila: The mediastinal contour is within normal limits. No mediastinal or hilar lymphadenopathy is present. The heart is normal in size. The pericardium appears unremarkable without evidence of pericardial effusion. The thoracic aorta is normal in caliber with no aneurysmal dilatation.

Chest wall and bones: No lytic or sclerotic osseous lesions are identified in the visualized thoracic skeleton. The soft tissues of the chest wall are unremarkable.

Upper abdomen (included on chest protocol): The visualized portions of the upper abdomen, including the liver dome and subphrenic spaces, appear unremarkable. No subphrenic fluid collections. No diaphragmatic nodularity or soft tissue thickening along either hemidiaphragm. The right hemidiaphragm, site of prior peritoneal implant requiring stripping at the time of debulking surgery, demonstrates no abnormal soft tissue nodularity or enhancement. The diaphragmatic surfaces appear smooth bilaterally.

---

ABDOMEN:

Liver: The liver is normal in size and morphology with homogeneous parenchymal enhancement. No focal hepatic lesions, hypodense masses, or enhancing nodules are identified. No capsular retraction or subcapsular thickening. The hepatic veins, portal vein, and inferior vena cava are patent. The bile ducts are not dilated. No perihepatic fluid or nodular hepatic surface implants.

Gallbladder: The gallbladder is present and normal in appearance without gallstones or wall thickening. No pericholecystic fluid.

Pancreas: The pancreas demonstrates normal morphology and enhancement throughout the head, neck, body, and tail. The main pancreatic duct is not dilated. No peripancreatic stranding, mass, or adenopathy.

Spleen: The spleen is normal in size and attenuation with homogeneous enhancement. No focal splenic lesions. No perisplenic fluid or nodularity.

Adrenal glands: Both adrenal glands are unremarkable in morphology and enhancement without adenoma or metastatic lesion.

Kidneys: Both kidneys are normal in size, contour, and enhancement. The previously identified 4 mm non-obstructing calculus in the right lower pole calyx, noted on the CT urogram performed approximately six months prior, is no longer convincingly identified, consistent with passage of the stone as clinically documented. No hydronephrosis or hydroureter bilaterally. No urothelial thickening or mass.

Retroperitoneum and mesentery: The retroperitoneum is clean without evidence of lymphadenopathy. The aorta and inferior vena cava are normal in caliber. No para-aortic, aortocaval, or pericaval lymph node enlargement is identified. The small bowel mesentery appears homogeneous and fatty without nodular soft tissue implants, mesenteric stranding, or infiltration. The omentum, which was resected at the time of debulking surgery, demonstrates expected post-surgical absence of the typical omental fat apron below the transverse colon; there is no recurrent omental soft tissue, mass, or caking. The mesenteric root and small bowel loops appear unremarkable. No bowel obstruction.

Peritoneal surfaces: Careful evaluation of the peritoneal surfaces is performed. The parietal peritoneum along the right and left paracolic gutters, previously sites of peritoneal implants at original presentation, is smooth without focal nodularity, soft tissue thickening, or enhancing deposits. The undersurface of the diaphragm bilaterally and the subhepatic and subphrenic spaces show no peritoneal deposits. No loculated or free intraperitoneal fluid. No ascites.

Right paracolic gutter: The right paracolic gutter is clear. The peritoneum here is smooth and follows the expected post-surgical contour without soft tissue implants.

Left paracolic gutter: Similarly unremarkable. No peritoneal thickening or nodularity.

Small bowel: Small bowel loops are of normal caliber and wall thickness without obstruction or mass effect. No serosal implants. The bowel wall enhances uniformly. No luminal narrowing.

Colon: The large bowel is normal in caliber. The sigmoid colon is unremarkable. No colonic wall thickening or pericolic fat stranding. The appendix was resected at the time of surgery and is absent.

---

PELVIS:

Post-surgical anatomy: The patient has undergone total abdominal hysterectomy and bilateral salpingo-oophorectomy. The uterus and adnexa are absent, as expected. The vaginal cuff appears well-healed and unremarkable without adjacent fluid collection, soft tissue mass, or enhancing nodule.

Vaginal cuff: The vaginal cuff is intact with no abnormal soft tissue thickening, mass, or enhancement at or adjacent to the surgical suture line.

Pelvic sidewalls: Both pelvic sidewalls are clear. There is no pelvic sidewall soft tissue mass, lymph node enlargement, or involvement of the pelvic musculature or vasculature. The previously noted right obturator fossa lymphocele, which measured 2.1 cm on post-operative imaging and had already decreased to 0.8 cm on the 6-week post-treatment scan, is no longer identified, consistent with complete resolution.

Lymph nodes: No pelvic lymphadenopathy is identified. The bilateral external iliac, internal iliac, obturator, and common iliac nodal stations are within normal size limits without suspicious morphology. The bilateral inguinal regions show no enlarged nodes.

Bladder: The urinary bladder is well-distended with a thin, smooth wall. No intraluminal mass or focal wall irregularity. The perivesical fat is clear.

Pelvic peritoneum and cul-de-sac: The cul-de-sac (rectovesical/rectovaginal space) contains no fluid, implant, or soft tissue deposit. The pelvic peritoneal surfaces are smooth without nodularity. No peritoneal enhancement or implant is identified along the pelvic floor, bladder reflection, or rectosigmoid serosa.

Rectum and sigmoid: The rectum and distal sigmoid are unremarkable without wall thickening, extrinsic compression, or adjacent lymphadenopathy.

Bony pelvis: The osseous structures of the pelvis are intact. No lytic or sclerotic lesions suspicious for metastatic disease. The sacrum, iliac wings, and proximal femora appear normal.

---

IMPRESSION:

1. No evidence of disease recurrence. The chest, abdomen, and pelvis have been carefully interrogated and no findings to suggest recurrent or metastatic high-grade serous ovarian carcinoma are identified. Specifically, there are no peritoneal implants, no omental nodularity or soft tissue, no ascites, no pelvic mass, no lymphadenopathy, and no parenchymal organ metastases.

2. The post-surgical pelvis is stable and unremarkable. The right obturator fossa lymphocele identified on prior studies has completely resolved.

3. The previously documented right renal calculus is no longer visualized, consistent with spontaneous passage as reported clinically.

4. The right hemidiaphragm, which underwent peritoneal stripping for a 1.5 cm implant at the time of primary debulking surgery, demonstrates no recurrent soft tissue nodularity or peritoneal thickening, now well over two years post-resection.

5. The peritoneal surfaces, including the omental bed, paracolic gutters, pelvic peritoneum, and subdiaphragmatic spaces, are all clear without any nodular deposits. This represents a continued and sustained complete imaging remission, consistent with the patient's persistently normalized CA-125 values throughout the maintenance therapy period.

Signed: Attending Radiologist, Body Imaging Section
Division of Abdominal Imaging and Oncologic Imaging
